key_insight: When multiple outputs share a hard latent feature, a joint compositional code can represent them with strictly fewer bits than coding each task separately, even if the outputs must remain orthogonal.
engineering_relevance: This gives a theoretical justification for shared backbones with task-specific heads in multi-task and multi-modal models under geometric constraints such as orthogonality, equivariance, or conservation laws.
method: Build a shared hard feature using a Rademacher-Haar wavelet series.
Attach Sawtooth-Walsh readouts to enforce orthogonality across output coordinates.
Prove sharp upper/lower bounds on description length for joint vs. separate approximation in uniform norm.
evidence_signal: The paper provides an information-theoretic approximation-theory analysis, deriving sharp bounds and a neural-network realization, rather than reporting empirical benchmark data.
watch_item: The separation is theoretical and relies on a specific compositional construction, so practical gains may depend on whether real tasks truly share a similarly hard latent feature.
paper_title: The Information-Theoretic Benefit of Shared Representations under Orthogonality Constraints
